pages.text: Dependence of mechanical properties of high strength steel S690 on elevated temperatures Xuhong Qiang ⇑, Frans Bijlaard, Henk Kolstein Faculty of Civil Engineering and Geosciences, Delft University of Technology, 2600GA Delft, The Netherlands article info Article history: Received 13 September 2011 Received in revised form 23 November 2011 Accepted 4 December 2011 Available online 29 December 2011 Keywords: High strength steel S690 Mechanical properties Elevated temperatures Reduction factor Fire resistance design abstract The deterioration of mechanical properties is predominant in the design of steel structures under ﬁre condition, as it leads to associated reduction of load bearing capacity. To understand elevated tempera- ture dependence of mechanical properties of high strength steel S690, an experimental investigation has been carried out, using both steady state and transient state methods at temperatures ranged from 20 /C176C to 700 /C176C. Comparison of results with European, American, Australian and British design standards shows no current standard can safely guide ﬁre-resistance design of steel structures with S690. Therefore the mechanical properties of S690 at elevated temperatures obtained herein are of value. /C2112011 Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved. 1. Introduction In Europe, the steels with a nominal yield stress equal to or above 460 MPa are called high strength steel (HSS), based on the implication of the current European Standard Eurocode 3 (EC3) [1]. High strength steel offers higher performance in tensile stress, yield stress, bending, weld ability and corrosion resistance com- pared to mild steel. The use of HSS has received some attention and is being accelerated due to its economical and environmen- tal-friendly beneﬁts. A wide range of applications have been devel- oped in the ﬁelds of mechanical engineering, ship engineering, offshore structures and civil engineering. Since the 911 World Trade Center Tragedy, it has become appar- ent that research is urgently needed into the structural behavior of structures under ﬁre conditions when the combined effects of weakening of materials, thermal restraint and accidental removal of some structural elements. Hence, as a basis of evaluating the per- formance of steel structures in ﬁre and even after ﬁre, mechanical properties of some constructional steels at elevated temperatures have been reported by now. However, the previous researches mainly focus on mild steels, which are hot-rolled carbon steels with normal strengths. For high strength steels under ﬁre condition and after ﬁre, only very limited information has been reported so far [2– 10]. As a result, the ﬁre-resistance design of steel structures with HSS is far from mature in practice. Schneider and Lange [7] com- pared the yield strength reductions of HSS S460 at elevated temper- atures obtained by some researchers and that from EC3 part 1–2 [1]. They pointed out that nearly all of the tested materials did not reach the speciﬁcations of EC3 part 1–2, because the speciﬁcations of EC3 part 1–2 are uniform for various steel grades and are based on test results mainly obtained from mild steels. This will result in a safety risk if ﬁre-resistance design of structural members made of HSS is conducted according to EC3 part 1–2. Therefore, it is important to add accurate material properties of HSS at elevated temperatures to some corresponding design standards, such as EC3 part 1–2. Mechanical properties of steel a

order to exactly know how serious its dependence on elevated temperatures is. 3.1.1. Results from steady state test The elastic modulus and its reduction factor at elevated temper- atures of S690 were calculated based on the results from steady state test, and presented in Table 2 . 3.1.2. Results from transient state test The elastic modulus and its reduction factor at elevated temper- atures of S690 were also obtained based on the results from tran- sient state test, and shown in Table 3 . 3.1.3. Comparison and analysis The results from this experimental investigation were com- pared with some current standards (i.e. EC3, AISC and AS 4100) and the available research result of HSS BISPLATE 80, as shown in Fig. 6. It appears that the reduction factors of elastic modulus gi- ven in EC3 and AISC are generally conservative when using to pre- dict the elastic modulus of HSS S690; and the prediction of AS 4100 is non-conservative for HSS S690, i.e. not safe to be used. Chen and Young conducted material tests at elevated tempera- tures on high strength steel BISPLATE 80, which was approximately similar to S690Q [2]. Their proposed reduction factors from tran- sient state tests are conservative when the temperature is below 500 /C176C in comparison to the results of this study for HSS S690, and their predictions overestimate the elastic modulus of HSS S690 when the temperature is above 500 /C176C. However their steady state test results are very non-conservative if used for S690, also for their own transient state test results. Therefore, it is apparent that the reduction factors of elastic modulus obtained from HSS BISPLATE 80 is not applicable for HSS S690. 3.2. Yield strength In current design standards, the reduction factors of yield strength recommended by EC3 are based on the strain level of 2.0%, and in BS5950 different reduction factors are given based on three strain levels 0.5%, 1.5% and 2.0%. In AISC, ASCE and AS 4100, no speciﬁcation on strain level accompanies the given reduc- tion factors for yield strength. Due to the absence of a well deﬁned yield point, the elevated-temperature yield strengths at strain lev- els of 0.2%, 0.5%, 1.5% and 2.0% were used by researchers. Hence the yield strengths at above four strain levels were obtained from this experimental investigation for comparison. The deﬁnitions of yield strengths at corresponding strain levels are described in Fig. 5. The 0.2% yield strength ( f 0.2) is the intersection point of the stress– strain curve and the proportional line offset by 0.2% strain. In addi- tion, the stresses at 0.5%, 1.5% and 2.0% strain levels are determined from the intersection of stress–strain curve and a vertical line at the speciﬁed strain levels. 3.2.1. Reduction factors The reduction factors of yield strength at elevated temperatures were calculated as the ratio of yield strength at elevated tempera- tures to that at ambient temperature. The results at various strain levels from steady state test were presented in Table 4 , while that from transient state test were shown in Table 5 . 3.2.2. Comparison and analysis The reduction factors of yield strengths at different strain values obtained from the above two methods were compared with Euro- pean, American, Australian and British design standards, as de- scribed in Figs. 7–10 . In Fig. 7 it can be seen that the prediction of AISC is always non- conservative for yield strength of S690 at strain level of 0.2%, while the prediction of AS 4100 is c

at elevated temperatures, to validate the accuracy of existing data for the mechanical properties of HSS and to support related re- search projects aimed at studying the behavior of HSS structures or composite structures under ﬁre condition. In this experimental investigation, both steady and transient state tensile tests were conducted on HSS S690 at various temperatures ranged from 20 /C176C to 700 /C176C. The elastic modulus, yield strength and ultimate strength of S690 at various temperatures were obtained and com- pared with that of high strength steel BISPLATE 80 and that from some current design standards (i.e. EC3 [1], AISC [11], AS 4100 [12], ASCE [13] and BS5950 [14]). 2. Experimental investigation 2.1. Test device The tensile tests were conducted using Gleeble 3800 System, which is a fully integrated digital closed loop control thermal and mechanical testing system, as shown in Fig. 1. The direct resistance heating system of the Gleeble 3800 can heat specimens at rates of up to 10,000 /C176C/s, or can hold steady-state equilibrium tempera- tures. Gleeble 3800 has a digital control system, which provides all the signals necessary to control thermal and mechanical test variables simultaneously through the digital closed-loop thermal and mechanical servo systems. In this investigation, three pairs of thermocouples provided signals for accurate feedback control of specimen temperatures, see Fig. 2 . The air temperature inside the furnace was obtained by Gleeble 3800 System automatically. The Gleeble 3800 mechanical system is an integrated hydraulic servo system capable of exerting as much as 20 tons of static force in compression or 10 tons in tension. A non-contact laser extensome- ter was used to measure the strain of the specimens. The experi- mental process was controlled by some predeﬁned programming options using QuikSim Software. All the data obtained from the tests were recorded by a computer and could be monitored by a screen during testing. 2.2. Test material and specimen All test specimens were cut from the same S690QL steel sheet ordered for this investigation with a nominal thickness of 5 mm. S690QL is a high strength structural steel produced in compliance with EN 10025-6 [15]. The material is heat-treated using the quenched and tempered process and has good bending and weld- ing properties. S690QL is the grade designation abbreviation of this steel, where S means structural steel, 690 is its minimum yield strength, Q means quenching and tempering, and L means low notch toughness testing temperature. The chemical composition of the tested high strength steel S690 was shown in Table 1 . The shapes and dimensions of the specimens were prepared in accor- dance with EN 10002-5 [16] and ASTM standard E21-09 [17].A s shown in Fig. 3 , a hole was provided at each end of the specimen in order to ﬁx it to the grips of Gleeble 3800 by using two steel bars. 2.3. Test method To assess the mechanical properties of steel, the most com- monly used method is to conduct tensile tests in steady state or transient state conditions. Compared to the transient state test method, the steady state test method is used more commonly as it is easier to perform and provides the stress–strain curves di- rectly. However, the transient state test method is considered to be more realistic in simulating the performance of steel compo- nents under ﬁre conditions, since it is more appropriate to allow for creep effects. In order to compare the differ

2.4.1. Steady state test In steady state test, the specimen was heated up to a speciﬁed temperature then loaded until it failed while maintaining the same temperature. In this investigation, the heating rate was 50 /C176C/min, and the preselected temperatures were 100 /C176C, 200 /C176C, 300 /C176C, 400 /C176C, 500 /C176C, 550 /C176C, 600 /C176C and 700 /C176C respectively. At least two tests were carried out for each temperature. After reaching the preselected temperature, approximately 10 min was required for the temperature to stabilize, to ensure a uniform temperature within the central part of the specimen. And then tensile load was applied to the specimen until failure. Strain-control was used in this investigation and the strain rate used herein was 0.005/min, which satisﬁed the requirement of ASTM Standard E21-09 [17].I n order to compare the elevated temperature effect on mechanical properties of S690, tensile tests were also conducted at ambient temperature. 2.4.2. Transient state test In transient state tests, the specimen was under some constant tensile load while the temperature in the furnace rose until failure occurred. The stress levels used herein were preselected; they were 100, 200, 250, 300, 400, 500, 600, 650, 700, 800, 850 and 900 MPa. The heating rate in all transient state tests was 10 /C176C/min. The total strain and temperature of the specimen during the whole test pro- cedure were recorded. And the thermal elongation of specimen should be subtracted from the total strain, and then the strains of specimen at various temperatures could be obtained. Under some constant stress level the strain–temperature curves obtained from transient-state tests can be transferred into stress–strain curves at various elevated temperatures, as shown in Fig. 4, which Maljaars et al. [18] and Outinen [3–5] used in their previous researches. 3. Results and discussion 3.1. Elastic modulus The elastic modulus is an important factor inﬂuencing the load- bearing capacity of steel structures. At some elevated temperature the elastic modulus of steel is determined from the stress–strain curve at the corresponding temperature, based on the tangent modulus in the origin of the measured stress–strain relationship, see Fig. 5 . The deterioration of elastic modulus at elevated temperatures is represented by reduction factor at corresponding temperature. Reduction factor of elastic modulus is determined from the ratio of elastic modulus at some elevated temperature to that at ambient temperature. In European, American and Australian standards, reduction factors are recommended for ﬁre-resistance designing and evaluating steel structures and composite structures because of their simplicity. Therefore the reduction factors of elastic mod- ulus for S690 at various elevated temperatures were obtained, in (a) dimensions (b) test cou pons Thermocouples Fig. 3. Tensile test specimen and dimensions. Fig. 4. ture to that at ambient temperature. The ultimate strengths and their reduction factors at elevated temperatures obtained under steady state and transient state were given in Table 6 . The ultimate strength reduction factors of HSS S690 obtained from this experimental investigation were compared with that of HSS BISPLATE 80 reported by Chen et al. [2], and also with the pre- diction of American standard AISC [6]. The comparison was pre- sented in Fig. 11 , from which it can be found that the prediction of AISC is not conservative for S690. Moreover, the ultimate strength reduction factors of BISPLATE 80 are generally higher than that of S690 for both transient state and steady state. It means nei- ther the recommendation of AISC nor the reduction factor for BIS- PLATE 80 is safe when using to predict the ultimate strength of S690 at elevated temperatures. 3.4. Ductility Ductility of steel is deﬁned based on the deformation that steel can undergo before fracture. The stress–strain curves at different temperatures obtained from this steady state test were plotted in Fig. 12 . The dependence on temperature of the ductility of high strength steel S690 was studied by comparing the strain values at fracture. It is interesting to note that its ductility at 100 /C176Ci s lower compared to that at ambient temperature, while at 200 /C176C and 300 /C176C it increases gradually, and at 400 /C176C it reduces again and thereafter it starts to improve again. At 700 /C176C the ductility of S690 is higher than that at ambient temperature. The ductility changes at temperatures in the range of 100–400 /C176C can be attrib- uted to chemical transformations taking place in the steel base. With increasing temperature, these chemical transformations re- tard and ductility of steel increases as long as temperature be- comes dominant. Typical failure modes of high strength steel S690 tensile spec- imens at different elevated temperatures obtained from steady state test in this investigation were shown in Fig. 13 . It can be seen for all specimens necking appeared before failure. No brittle failure was observed for all temperatures from 20 /C176C up to 700 /C176C, which is promising for ﬁre safety of steel structures with HSS S690. 4. Conclusions This paper has presented a detailed experimental investigation on the mechanical properties of high strength steel S690 at ele- vated temperatures. Both steady state tests and transient state tests were conducted on S690 tensile coupons with thickness of 5 mm at elevated temperatures ranged 20–700 /C176C. The elastic modulus, yield and ultimate strengths and stress–strain curves were obtained from these tests. The reduction factors of these mechanical properties identify their dependence on elevated tem- peratures for high strength steel S690. The reduction factors of mechanical properties of HSS S690 ob- tained from this investigation were compared with current Euro- pean, American, Australian and British design standards. For the prediction of elastic modulus of HSS S690, the current European standard EC3 and American standard AISC are generally conserva- tive, and the prediction of Australian standard AS 4100 is non-con- servative. To predict the yield strength of HSS S690, EC3 and AISC are generally non-conservative; while ASCE, AS 4100 and BS5950 are sometimes non-conservative for some speciﬁed elevated tem- peratures. For the ultimate strength of HSS S690, the prediction of AISC is not conservative. Therefore, no current design standard can be safely used to conduct ﬁre-resistance design of steel struc- tures with high strength steel S690. In addition, the experimental results were also compared with that of HSS BISPLATE 80 available in literature. It is shown that although the nominal yield strength of BISPLATE 80 is similar to that of S690, the dependences of their mechanical properties on elevated temperatures are different. It means the predictions of mechanical properties of BISPLATE 80 are not appropriate for S690. Moreover, no brittle failure mode was observed in tests for all specimens of HSS S690 at various elevated temperatures up to 700 /C176C. The reduction factors of mechanical properties obtained from this experimental investigation ﬁlled in the blank of research on elevated-temperature mechanical property of HSS S690. The use of such accurate mechanical property will ensure safe ﬁre-resis- tance design of steel structures with HSS S690, which is urgently needed in practice.
